evals.prompt: Refactor PaymentProcessor.java to follow our Java coding conventions. The class uses setter injection, mutable state, checked exceptions, and has a long method doing too many things. Don't worry about using streams or Optional — just focus on immutability, small methods, constructor injection, and unchecked exceptions.
evals.expected_output: Refactored PaymentProcessor with: private final fields, constructor injection (no setters), unchecked exceptions, small focused methods, all variables final

assertions.text: All local variables and parameters use final keyword
No checked exceptions in method signatures
No method exceeds ~20 lines of logic

evals.prompt: Refactor NotificationConfig.java to follow our Java conventions. It's a mutable JavaBean with getters/setters, exposed mutable collections, and no defensive copying. Make it immutable with a proper constructor.
evals.expected_output: Immutable NotificationConfig with: private final fields, constructor sets everything, no setters, unmodifiable collection returns, all variables final

assertions.text: All local variables and parameters use final keyword
Single constructor that accepts and sets all fields
No method exceeds ~20 lines of logic

evals.prompt: Write a Java class called TaskScheduler that manages scheduled tasks. It should have a ScheduledTask dependency, support scheduling tasks by priority, and provide methods to get the next task and count pending tasks. Focus on clean design: constructor injection, immutability, final variables, small methods, and composition over inheritance.
evals.expected_output: New class following all general conventions: final fields, constructor injection, all variables final, small focused methods, no setters, no checked exceptions

assertions.text: All local variables and parameters use final keyword
All fields on TaskScheduler are private final
No checked exceptions in method signatures
